``` ::: ::: :::
:::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} ## Parole Eligibility
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

How is Parole Release Eligibility Determined?

Crimes in Tennessee have both a class (that determines the broad continuum of pun- ishment) that ranges from A through E and a guidelines- based classification (which is a slightly narrower range within each class). The standard classifications are Range I (Standard), Range II (Multiple), and Range III (Persistent); however, there is also the possibility of an “especially mitigated” sentence or a “career offender” sentence. Statutory law determines the class of an offense, but judges determine the classification of the offense at sentencing, using the sentencing guidelines. The classification determines both the length of the total sentence and the percentage of the sentence that must be served before eligibility for parole.


People who were Eligible for Parole

In 2020, White, non-Hispanic people constituted the most number of people eligible for parole but still in prison, accounting for 57% (4,689 people) of the parole-eligible prison population.



In 2020, there were 1,503 people who were incarcerated for a parole revocation and were eligible for parole but not yet released from prison.

``` ::: :::
::: {.cell} :::
## Prison Population
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Prison Population

In 2020, there were 8,244 people who were eligible for parole but not released from prison, constituting 30% of the parole-eligible prison population.




``` ::: :::

## Offenses ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Most Serious Sentenced Offense

In 2020, there were 5,115 people who were parole eligible but still in prison for non-violent offenses, accounting for 62% of the parole-eligible prison population.
